summary: Dan Shapiro recounts his attempt to use generative AI tools as a personal fashion consultant after his wife suggested he needed new clothes. He tested multiple image generators to visualize startup CEO outfits, then used Sora to create a video of himself in various looks, which actually produced promising results. When he tried to go further by using ChatGPT's agent mode to autonomously shop for clothes and generate a Sora video, the system failed after thirty minutes of effort. The post serves as a lighthearted illustration of where AI capabilities currently stand — impressive at generation, but not yet ready for multi-step autonomous tasks.
key_points: Image generators across providers converge on a narrow stereotype of what a 'startup CEO' looks like, including Chinese models defaulting to white males
Sora proved surprisingly capable at generating coherent fashion combinations and styling choices with minimal prompting
ChatGPT's agent mode failed when tasked with the multi-step workflow of shopping, carting items, and generating a Sora video
There remains a significant gap between AI's generative capabilities and its ability to execute complex, multi-tool autonomous workflows
money_quotes: My wife pointed out that most of my shirts predate the pandemic, and it was time to shop for something new.
So, like most things I don't enjoy, I decided to try and delegate it to AI.
They definitely had a type. (Even the chinese models made white dudes)
I was impressed. Some of those looked nice? Maybe like something I'd actually wear?
I checked with my fashion consultant/spouse, the one who actually has to look at me, and she concurred.
It spent half an hour and had me log into Sora… and then it was done.
I guess we have a ways to go before we get to AGI.
key_insight: Current AI excels at creative generation tasks like styling and visualization but breaks down when asked to autonomously chain together multiple real-world actions.
